text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr Anjali Rao
Eastfield Medical Practice

14 Meadowbank Road

Bristol BS7 1JP
Dear Dr Anjali Rao
Martin Ellis, DOB: 09-03-1986, Hospital No: H937521 NHS No. 7483201965
12C Larkspur Court, Bishopston, Bristol BS7 8QG 

Diagnoses/summary: Drug-resistant focal epilepsy (temporal lobe features), with stress- and intercurrent infection-related exacerbations. Prior EEGs suggest left temporal interictal discharges; neuropsychology consistent with dominant temporal involvement.

MRI brain 2018 and 2023 reported no clear epileptogenic lesion; mild nonspecific white matter change. ECG 2024 normal sinus rhythm.

Previous antiseizure medicines: carbamazepine (rash), lamotrigine (exacerbation of aura frequency), levetiracetam (mood irritability), valproate (weight gain, hair thinning), clobazam (sedation), lacosamide (dizziness), oxcarbazepine (hyponatraemia), zonisamide (cognitive slowing). Not trialled: perampanel, topiramate, brivaracetam, cenobamate, pregabalin, tiagabine, rufinamide, acetazolamide.

I reviewed Martin Ellis in clinic today, attending alone. He reports that his focal impaired-awareness seizures continue to cluster around periods of intercurrent viral symptoms; in his words, "seizures happen after illness" and are typically preceded by a rising epigastric aura, déjà vu, and a brief pause in speech. Outside these episodes he may go several weeks with only occasional simple auras. His partner has observed brief oral automatisms and right-hand fumbling lasting 60–90 seconds, followed by 10–15 minutes of fatigue. He denies convulsive activity in the past year. Sleep fragmentation and work-related stress were noted as additional potential triggers. He no longer drives and has notified DVLA.

Current medicines: brivaracetam 100 mg twice daily (initiated by local team 4 months ago), sertraline 50 mg daily, vitamin D 1000 IU daily, omeprazole 20 mg daily. He uses PRN midazolam buccal for prolonged events (rarely required). No alcohol misuse; caffeine modest. No known cardiac disease; family history negative for epilepsy. Weight stable; BMI 24.5. Bloods from August 2025: FBC, U&Es, LFTs, TFTs within reference. 

Examination today: alert, oriented, speech fluent; cranial nerves intact, no focal motor deficit, Romberg negative, gait steady. No stigmata of phenytoin exposure. BP 122/74, pulse 68 regular. 

Discussion and plan: We reviewed options given persistent focal epilepsy and the clear pattern that "seizures happen after illness"; we discussed preventative strategies around intercurrent infections (hydration, sleep preservation, early antipyretic use as tolerated) and seizure action planning. We agreed to maintain brivaracetam at current dose for now and to introduce topiramate as an adjunct, with cautious titration owing to cognitive side effects and risk of weight loss and kidney stones. I explained potential adverse effects including paraesthesiae, dysgeusia, mood change, glaucoma risk, and metabolic acidosis; advised good hydration and to report visual changes urgently. We also discussed the role of perampanel or cenobamate as alternatives if needed, ambulatory EEG during a symptomatic week, and consideration of epilepsy surgery work-up if drug resistance persists. Vagus nerve stimulation noted as a future option if medication strategies fail. 

- Start topiramate 25 mg at night for 1 week, then 25 mg twice daily for 1 week, then 50 mg morning/25 mg night for 1 week, then 50 mg twice daily; hold further increases pending nurse review. 
- Continue brivaracetam 100 mg twice daily. 
- Buccal midazolam to remain as rescue; no routine changes. 
- Sick-day guidance provided: at onset of viral symptoms, prioritise fluid intake, temperature control, and rest; if breakthrough clusters occur, contact epilepsy nurse line. 
- Bloods (U&Es, bicarbonate) in 4–6 weeks after reaching 50 mg twice daily. 
- Seizure diary to document patterns around illnesses; partner to video (with consent) brief events for review. 
- DVLA advice reiterated; avoid swimming/bathing alone and heights. 

Follow-up: Nurse telephone review in 4 weeks to consider further topiramate titration. Clinic review with me in 4 months, or sooner if deterioration. I have provided written information on medication side effects and emergency contact pathways. 

Please continue repeat prescriptions and routine monitoring locally. Many thanks for your ongoing care.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: He reports that his focal impaired-awareness seizures continue to cluster around periods of intercurrent viral symptoms; ... Outside these episodes he may go several weeks with only occasional simple auras.

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter clearly states the patient continues to have focal impaired-awareness seizures that cluster around periods of intercurrent viral symptoms, with occasional simple auras between illnesses. However, no explicit numeric frequency (per day/week/month/year) is provided. There is mention that outside illness episodes he may go several weeks with only occasional simple auras, but 'occasional' and clustering after illnesses do not yield a calculable frequency. No counts per cluster are provided either. Therefore, the specific frequency cannot be determined from the letter.
seizure_frequency_number: He reports that his focal impaired-awareness seizures continue to cluster around periods of intercurrent viral symptoms; ... Outside these episodes he may go several weeks with only occasional simple auras.
